draft.introduction: Emperor Julian’s Hymn to King Helios (362 CE) is the most sophisticated theological defense of solar worship from late antiquity. Julian, the last pagan emperor, presents the Sun as the visible image of the transcendent Good, mediating between intelligible and material realms.

translation.text: What I am now about to say I consider to be of the greatest importance for all things that breathe and move upon the earth, and also for the blessed gods who receive their light from on high. I mean that King Helios is the common benefactor of all, who occupies the middle region among the intellectual gods, bringing harmony, synthesis, perfection, and unifying power.
translation.original_text: ὃ δὲ νῦν εἰπεῖν μέλλω παντὸς μάλιστα ἔχειν ηγοῦμαι

translator_notes.note: Julian’s theology places Helios in the mediating position between the intelligible and visible realms—a role structurally identical to the Logos in Christian theology or the World Soul in Plotinus. The hymn reveals how deeply Neoplatonic categories had penetrated late pagan religion, making the conflict with Christianity a dispute between philosophical cousins.
